cards._comment: TEMPLATE — copy this entry to add a hand-picked card. Set draft:false (or remove it) to ship. 'slug' must be stable + unique (it becomes the deep-link id curated:<slug>); change it and old shared links break. Only title, summary, and source.url are required; the rest are optional. JSON has no comments, so this draft entry IS the documentation — leave it here, it never ships.
cards.title: Card title (one line)
cards.summary: One or two sentences shown on the card face. Keep it tight — long summaries get trimmed.
cards.body: Optional longer prose shown when the card is opened. Use \n for line breaks.

cards.title: Handwriting wires the brain differently than typing
cards.summary: Students who take notes by hand tend to remember ideas better than laptop typists — and a 2024 EEG study found handwriting lights up far more of the brain. But the headline has an honest asterisk.
cards.body:
The classic study is Mueller & Oppenheimer (2014), "The Pen Is Mightier Than the Keyboard." Laptop note-takers tended to transcribe lectures word for word; longhand writers, unable to keep up, had to summarize and reframe in their own words — and scored better on conceptual questions afterward.

The honest asterisk: a 2021 multi-lab replication reproduced the verbatim-transcription difference but NOT the learning advantage. So "handwriting makes you understand more" is shakier than the popular version suggests.

The freshest angle is brain activity, not test scores. A 2024 EEG study (Van der Meer & Van der Weel, NTNU) found that forming letters by hand produces far more widespread connectivity across the brain than typing. The slow, looping, ever-varying motion of writing seems to engage memory and learning networks that pressing identical keys does not.

source.name: Frontiers in Psychology
source.author: Van der Weel & Van der Meer (2024)
